summary: This is an advice column post where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from why she loves living in New York City despite its crowds and expense, to handling unresponsive academic collaborators. The post opens with playful bragging about her crepe-making skills before diving into reader mail. The meatiest content involves her passionate defense of urban life—embracing crowds, diverse subway encounters, and even appreciating rudeness as a form of honest human interaction. She also provides pragmatic advice to an academic dealing with collaborators who've gone completely silent, suggesting direct ultimatums while maintaining compassion for unknown personal circumstances.
key_points: O'Neil articulates a counter-cultural appreciation for urban density, dirt, and even rudeness as authentic human experiences compared to suburban passive-aggression
She frames subway delays and crowded conditions as opportunities for genuine human connection rather than inconveniences
On academic collaboration, she advises pragmatic directness: give clear deadlines and consequences while withholding resentment since you don't know others' circumstances
The column reveals her personality through lifestyle details—no car ownership, weather machismo, sending kids out independently in NYC
She expresses frustration at running low on reader questions, insisting all submissions must be actual questions
money_quotes: I like things crowded and dirty. I like people of all ages and races and ethnicities sweating on each other in the subway.
That's why I like it when the subway stops for an hour in the tunnel and we all end up missing whatever appointments we had and we talk to each and behave like human beings. That's New York!
I don't know how people in suburbia deal with hostility! Maybe through those commuter traffic jams? Too passive aggressive to me, I want it to be face-to-face.
Just seeing other people be pushy and pointy helps me find my zen.
Chances are, though, they just get overwhelmed with other projects and don't quite want to shit and don't quite want to get off the pot either. It's your job to make them decide which one to do.
No reason to hold back your publications either.
key_insight: Authentic human friction—whether in crowded subways or difficult collaborations—is preferable to the passive-aggressive avoidance of suburban or academic politeness.
